findings.summary: Two distinct Model classes exist at src/core/model.py and src/models/base.py with incompatible signatures, creating a permanent identity fork in the codebase
evidence: src/core/model.py defines Model.__init__(self, name=None, parameters=None) with full case dispatch logic, precision weights, connections list, and _case_configurations dict
src/models/base.py defines a separate Model.__init__(self, name: str, case: Case = Case.NOMINATIVE) with a data_buffer dict and no precision or connections attributes
src/models/base.py re-exports Case from src.core.model (line 12: 'from src.core.model import Case') but redefines Model, meaning Model in src.models is NOT a subclass of Model in src.core
src/__init__.py exposes Model from src.core (via 'from .core import Model'), while src/models/__init__.py exposes a different Model from src.models.base
src/models/linear_regression.py inherits from src.models.base.Model (via 'from .base import Model'), so LinearRegressionModel is completely disjoint from the core Model hierarchy
src/models/insect/base.py imports ActiveInferenceModel from src.core.active_inference (which inherits core Model) but imports Case from src.core.model directly, bypassing models.base entirely
findings.suggestion: Collapse src/models/base.py into src/core/model.py. The lightweight Model in models/base.py should either be deleted and replaced with a direct import of src.core.model.Model, or merged by extending core Model. All subclasses currently under src/models/ should inherit from the single canonical Model in src.core.

findings.summary: The Case enum is re-exported independently from src.core.model, src.cases.__init__, src.models.base, and src.models.__init__, creating four parallel import paths for the same object
evidence: src/core/model.py: canonical definition of Case
src/models/base.py line 12: 're-exports Case from src.core.model for unified identity' but this comment is misleading since it makes a second canonical path
src/models/__init__.py line 6: 'from .base import Model, Case' — exposes Case through models package
src/cases/__init__.py line 29: 'from src.core.model import Case' — third re-export site

Consumers across 32 files use inconsistent import paths (some import from src.core.model directly, some from src.models, some from src.cases), confirmed by grep count showing 32 files importing from src.core.model alone
findings.suggestion: Define Case once in src/core/model.py. Remove the re-export from src/models/base.py and src/cases/__init__.py. Consumers should import Case from a single documented location (src.core or src directly via src.__init__).

findings.summary: src/core/model.py has 57 importers including modules from cases, models, transformations, visualization, and examples — any change to it causes cascading breakage across the entire codebase
evidence: 57 importer count (from blind packet) is the highest in the repo by a wide margin
Modules from at least 6 distinct subsystems (cases, models, transformations, visualization, examples, scripts) all reach directly into src.core.model

Insect case modules (swarm, caste, substrate, pheromonal, metamorphic, stigmergic) each import Case from src.core.model with absolute path — meaning a leaf-layer domain module has a hard coupling to the core kernel
src/core/model.py line 8 calls logging.basicConfig at module import time, which is a global side effect executed on first import across all 57 dependents
findings.suggestion: Extract Case enum into a dedicated src/core/case.py with zero dependencies. This decouples the enum (needed everywhere) from Model (needed in fewer places), reducing the blast radius of each file independently.

findings.summary: src/core/model.py calls logging.basicConfig() at module import time, which is a global side effect that mutates the root logger for every process that imports any CEREBRUM module
evidence: src/core/model.py line 8: 'logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format=...)' is executed unconditionally at module scope
This file has 57 importers, meaning this side effect fires on first import in every consumer process
At least 15 other files also call logging.basicConfig at module or function scope, creating unpredictable handler stacking depending on import order
findings.suggestion: Remove logging.basicConfig from module scope in src/core/model.py and all other library modules. Move any logging configuration into an explicit setup function callable by application entry points only (scripts, examples, tests). Library code should use logging.getLogger(__name__) without configuring the root handler.

findings.summary: beyond_cerebrum/ is a parallel top-level source tree with its own src/, formalisms, and operations modules but has no documented integration boundary with the main src/ package
evidence: beyond_cerebrum/ mirrors the structure of src/ with its own src/analysis, src/formalisms, src/operations, src/visualization subdirectories
pyproject.toml hatch.build.targets.wheel packages=['src'] — beyond_cerebrum is not included in the wheel, making its relationship to the distributed package undefined

No imports found from beyond_cerebrum into src/ or vice versa (grep returned zero matches), meaning the two trees are entirely decoupled at runtime but structurally parallel — suggests duplication or a future integration that has not been formalized

beyond_cerebrum/src/formalisms/structures.py defines FeatureStructure and graph primitives that parallel functionality in src/lexicon/graph/assembler.py, indicating potential cross-package concept duplication
findings.suggestion: Either formally integrate beyond_cerebrum as a src subpackage (e.g., src/formalisms/) with explicit boundary docs, or move it to a completely separate repository. Add it to pyproject.toml packages list if it is meant to be distributed. The current layout creates navigability confusion and undefined distribution scope.

findings.summary: src/models/ is supposed to be a domain model layer but contains a base.py that redefines the framework-level Model class, creating an ambiguous layer identity
evidence: src/models/__init__.py exports Model and Case alongside domain classes (CaseDefinitions, LinearRegressionModel), blurring whether src.models is an application-domain layer or a core infrastructure layer
src/models/base.py has a docstring 'Base Model and Case Definitions for CEREBRUM / Defines fundamental structures for linguistic case modeling' which is the same claim made by src/core/model.py
A caller importing from src.models gets a fundamentally different Model than one importing from src.core, with no type relationship between them
findings.suggestion: Remove src/models/base.py entirely. src/models/__init__.py should import Model from src.core, not re-export a locally-defined shadow. The models package should contain only concrete model implementations that extend src.core.Model.

findings.summary: Example scripts exist in both src/examples/ (10 files) and src/lexicon/examples/ (2 files), with no navigational signposting between them
evidence: src/examples/ contains at minimum: animal_agent.py, comprehensive_insect_simulation.py, environment.py, hvac_system.py, insect_simulation_example.py, insect_visualization_example.py, simplified_insect_simulation.py, streamlined_animal_agent.py, thermostat.py — nine example files in one flat directory with mixed concerns
src/lexicon/examples/ holds additional examples (basic_usage.py, process_file.py) scoped to the lexicon subsystem but living in a different location than src/examples/
src/examples/insect_simulation_example.py imports from src.models.insect.* with absolute paths, meaning it is tightly affinity-coupled to src.models but placed outside it
findings.suggestion: Consolidate examples under a single top-level examples/ directory outside src/ (not installable), or create domain-scoped example directories co-located with each subsystem. Remove src/examples from the installable wheel package — examples should not be in the distributable src/ tree.

findings.summary: Visualization code is split across src/visualization/ and src/lexicon/visualization/ with overlapping responsibilities and no shared base
evidence: src/visualization/ contains 13 files including insect-specific animation, behavior, case, and neural visualizers

src/lexicon/visualization/ contains 5 files including graph_visualizer.py, animated_graph.py, interactive_graph_visualizer.py — all graph-visualization utilities that share matplotlib/networkx/plotly as rendering dependencies with src/visualization/
Both trees import networkx independently (confirmed by grep across both paths), indicating no shared visualization utility layer
imageio is used in both src/utils/animation.py and src/lexicon/visualization/animated_graph.py without a shared animation helper
findings.suggestion: Create a single src/visualization/base.py with shared rendering utilities. Move lexicon-specific graph views into src/visualization/lexicon/ or keep them in src/lexicon/visualization/ but extract a shared src/visualization/common/ that both import from, eliminating the duplicate dependency surface.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/core/config.py imports torch at runtime inside a function but torch is not listed in any pyproject.toml dependency group, making the implicit dependency undeclared
evidence: src/lexicon/core/config.py lines 37-52: configure_torch_environment() imports torch inside the function body and calls torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.cuda.empty_cache(), torch.cuda.get_device_name()
pyproject.toml lists no torch in dependencies, dev, lexicon, llm, or all extras
src/lexicon/core/environment.py also imports torch (confirmed by grep: 'Found 2 files: src/lexicon/core/environment.py, src/lexicon/core/config.py')
src/lexicon/core/config.py has 25 importers (second highest in the repo per blind packet), meaning torch is implicitly required by 25 modules without any declared dependency
findings.suggestion: Either add torch as an optional dependency in pyproject.toml under a new 'torch' or 'gpu' extras group and guard all torch imports with try/except ImportError, or remove the torch dependency from the lexicon core and implement device selection using a lighter-weight path that degrades gracefully without torch.

findings.summary: scikit-learn is declared only in the dev optional extras group but is imported in src/models/linear_regression.py which is part of the main installable package
evidence: pyproject.toml line 44: 'scikit-learn>=1.3.0' appears under [project.optional-dependencies] dev, not under core dependencies

src/models/linear_regression.py lines 10-11: 'from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error' and 'from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression' are unconditional top-level imports
src/models/__init__.py line 8: 'from .linear_regression import LinearRegressionModel' — this causes sklearn to be imported at package initialization time whenever src.models is imported
A user who installs cerebrum without the dev extras will get an ImportError on 'import src.models'
findings.suggestion: Move scikit-learn to the core dependencies list in pyproject.toml, or move LinearRegressionModel to an optional subpackage (e.g., src/models/contrib/linear_regression.py) that is not imported in src/models/__init__.py. If sklearn is truly optional, guard the import in linear_regression.py with try/except and add a dedicated 'ml' extras group.

findings.summary: The llm extras group declares both aiohttp and requests as dependencies, and the openrouter client likely uses one or both alongside the openai SDK, indicating redundant HTTP client pull
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 53-56: llm extras declares openai>=1.0.0, python-dotenv>=1.0.0, aiohttp>=3.8.0, requests>=2.28.0
openai>=1.0.0 bundles httpx as its HTTP transport layer, meaning three separate HTTP client libraries (requests, aiohttp, httpx via openai) are pulled into the llm extras
aiohttp and requests serve the same purpose (synchronous and async HTTP) and are both heavy dependencies; having both alongside an SDK that already bundles its own transport is redundant
findings.suggestion: Audit src/llm/ to determine which HTTP calls actually use requests vs aiohttp vs openai's internal transport. Consolidate to the minimum set: if all LLM calls go through the openai SDK, drop both requests and aiohttp from the llm extras. If direct HTTP calls are needed, pick one library and standardize on it.

findings.summary: Case handler classes follow inconsistent protocols across sibling modules: NominativeCase and AccusativeCase lack PRECISION and VALID_TRANSITIONS class constants present in AblativeCase, LocativeCase, GenitiveCase, InstrumentalCase, and VocativeCase.
evidence: AblativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 1.1`, LocativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 0.9`, GenitiveCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 1.0`, InstrumentalCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 0.8`, VocativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 2.0` — but NominativeCase and AccusativeCase have no PRECISION constant.

AblativeCase (line 27): `VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.NOMINATIVE]`, LocativeCase (line 27): `VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.ABLATIVE]` — neither NominativeCase, AccusativeCase, InstrumentalCase, GenitiveCase, VocativeCase, nor DativeCase define this attribute.

AccusativeCase exposes two extra static methods not present on any other case class: `accept_updates` (line 117) and NominativeCase has no analogue. InstrumentalCase uniquely adds `execute_operation` (line 130) and `register_operation` (line 154). DativeCase lacks `get_parameters`, which is present on six of eight sibling classes.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared abstract base class or Protocol for all case handlers, declaring required class-level constants (PRECISION, VALID_TRANSITIONS) and a standard method surface (apply, process_update, calculate_free_energy, get_parameters). Each case module must then satisfy the protocol, eliminating the per-class drift.

findings.summary: CaseManager.case_handlers only registers NominativeCase and AccusativeCase, silently falling back to bare model.case assignment for the other six cases, contradicting the pattern established by the full eight-case class hierarchy.
evidence: Lines 28–32 of case_manager.py: `self.case_handlers = { Case.NOMINATIVE: NominativeCase, Case.ACCUSATIVE: AccusativeCase, # Add other cases as they're implemented }` — the comment acknowledges the gap, but the remaining six case handler classes exist and are exported from `__init__.py`.
Lines 77–83: `transform_case` falls through to `model.case = target_case` for all non-registered cases, bypassing the handler classes entirely.

Lines 139–146: `process_update` similarly falls back to `model.update(data)` for unregistered cases, meaning DativeCase, GenitiveCase, InstrumentalCase, AblativeCase, LocativeCase, and VocativeCase handlers are dead code from the manager's perspective.
findings.suggestion: Populate case_handlers with all eight case classes at construction time. If a case class is not yet production-ready, mark it explicitly with a feature flag rather than leaving the registry empty and silently bypassing the handler layer.

findings.summary: InsectModel introduces a redundant `self.current_case` attribute (line 92 of base.py) alongside the inherited `Model._case` / `Model.case` property, creating two parallel case-tracking fields that can diverge silently.
evidence: InsectModel.__init__ line 92: `self.current_case = initial_case` — a plain instance attribute set directly, not via `Model.case` setter.
InsectModel internally reads `self.current_case` throughout (lines 337, 343, 353-354, 388, 584, 701), while `Model.case` property is backed by `self._case`. The two can differ if only one is updated.
Model.case setter (model.py lines 60-73) maintains `_case_history` and calls `_apply_case_transformation`; bypassing it with `self.current_case` skips both side-effects.
findings.suggestion: Remove `self.current_case` from InsectModel and route all reads/writes through the inherited `self.case` property. If additional case-transition logic is needed, override `_apply_case_transformation` rather than maintaining a parallel field.

findings.summary: src/core/model.py calls logging.basicConfig at module import time, which is a global side-effect that pollutes the root logger for any consumer of the library.
evidence: model.py line 8: `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')` executes unconditionally on import.

active_inference.py (line 8) correctly uses `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` without calling basicConfig. The root module establishes a contradictory pattern that every downstream importer inherits.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `logging.basicConfig` call from model.py. Add `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` and let the application entry point configure the root handler. All 57 importers of model.py currently receive the misconfigured root handler on first import.

findings.summary: Case modules mix bare `logging.warning/logging.info` calls with the module-level `logger` pattern used elsewhere in the project, creating two different logging namespaces within the same package.
evidence: nominative.py line 40: `logging.info(...)`, line 107: `logging.warning(...)` — uses the root logger.
accusative.py line 39: `logging.info(...)`, line 76: `logging.info(...)`, line 111: `logging.warning(...)` — same pattern.
insect/base.py line 17: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)`, line 118: `logger.info(...)` — uses named module logger.
active_inference.py line 8: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` — uses named module logger. No case module defines a named logger despite emitting many log messages.
findings.suggestion: Add `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` at module level in each case module and replace all bare `logging.X` calls with `logger.X`. This gives per-module filtering granularity and is consistent with the rest of the codebase.

findings.summary: All eight case handler calculate_free_energy methods catch bare Exception, log a warning, and return a hardcoded default float — silently swallowing all errors including programming mistakes, with no way for callers to distinguish a computed result from a fallback.
evidence: nominative.py lines 96-108: `try: ... except Exception as e: logging.warning(...); return default_fe` — default_fe=1.0.
accusative.py lines 99-112: identical pattern — default_fe=0.5.
ablative.py lines 95-106: identical pattern — default_fe=1.0 but uses `AblativeCase.PRECISION` multiplier before the exception, so the default does not reflect the same scale.

All eight case files follow this identical catch-and-return-default pattern, meaning AttributeError on a missing attribute, TypeError from a bad numpy operation, and MemoryError from a large array all produce the same silent warning log and identical numeric return.

CaseManager.calculate_free_energy (case_manager.py lines 185-206) has no exception handling at all, so if free_energy() raises NotImplementedError from the base Model, it propagates uncaught — inconsistent with the swallowing pattern in every case class.
findings.suggestion: Narrow the catch to expected numeric/attribute errors (e.g., `except (AttributeError, ValueError, np.linalg.LinAlgError)`). Return a sentinel such as `float('nan')` or a typed Result so callers can detect the fallback. For `CaseManager.calculate_free_energy`, add a catch for `NotImplementedError` to match the handler behaviour, or document the inconsistency explicitly.

findings.summary: The core subsystem raises generic built-in exceptions (NotImplementedError, ValueError, TypeError, RuntimeError) while the lexicon subsystem maintains a full typed exception hierarchy (LexiconError → ConfigurationError, APIError, ProcessingError, etc.), with no bridging at subsystem boundaries.
evidence: model.py lines 123, 135: raises bare `NotImplementedError` with no custom exception type.
linear_regression.py lines 69, 80, 160: raises bare `ValueError` strings.
src/llm/OpenRouter/openrouter.py lines 212, 252: raises bare `Exception("Circuit breaker is OPEN...")` — the most degenerate possible error type.

lexicon/core/exceptions.py: defines 12 typed exception classes (LexiconError, ConfigurationError, APIError, ModelError, InputError, GraphError, StorageError, ProcessingError, RateLimitError, AuthenticationError, TimeoutError, ParsingError).
lexicon/core/engine.py line 154: raises `ValueError` inside the lexicon subsystem rather than `ConfigurationError`, breaching its own hierarchy.
src/llm/ollama/client.py lines 158-165: raises `OllamaConnectionError` and `OllamaError` (a third, separate error hierarchy not visible in lexicon/core/exceptions.py).
findings.suggestion: Define a top-level `CerebrumError` hierarchy for the core subsystem mirroring what lexicon already has. Retire bare `Exception(...)` raises in the LLM layer; map them into the appropriate hierarchy. At lexicon/core/engine.py:154, replace `ValueError` with `ConfigurationError` to stay consistent with the declared hierarchy.

findings.summary: InstrumentalCase.execute_operation returns None when the requested operation is not found on the model, instead of raising an error, creating a silent failure mode.
evidence: instrumental.py lines 144-151: `if hasattr(model, operation) and callable(getattr(model, operation)): ... return result` / `else: logging.warning(...); return None` — callers receive None with no indication that the operation was not found.

This contrasts with model.py lines 123, 135 which raise NotImplementedError for unimplemented interface methods — the two failure modes for a missing capability are inconsistent within the same framework.
findings.suggestion: Raise a specific error (e.g., `AttributeError` or a custom `OperationNotFoundError`) instead of returning None. If None-return is required for backward compatibility, at minimum raise in strict mode and document the silent-failure contract explicitly.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/visualization/animated_graph.py swallows at least six distinct Exception catches with only a log.error or log.warning and no propagation, meaning animation generation failures are invisible to callers.
evidence: Lines 50-51: `except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Failed to create graph construction animation: {e}")` — function returns None.
Lines 58-59, 66-67, 179-180, 271-272, 319-320, 342-343: all follow the same catch-and-log pattern with no re-raise.
Callers cannot distinguish a successfully produced animation from a silently failed one without inspecting side-effects (presence of output files).
findings.suggestion: Either re-raise after logging (`raise`) or return a typed result/success indicator that callers can check. Where failure is expected and recoverable, document it explicitly rather than silently returning None.

findings.summary: NominativeCase.apply() is a pure passthrough that only sets model.case and logs; it adds no policy over the base setter.
evidence: NominativeCase.apply() (nominative.py:23-41) sets model.case = Case.NOMINATIVE and calls logging.info, nothing more.

CaseManager.transform_case() (case_manager.py:66-83) calls handler.apply(model) for NOMINATIVE/ACCUSATIVE, then falls back to model.case = target_case for all other cases — making the handler path equivalent to the fallback for the two cases that do have handlers.

CaseManager only registers handlers for NOMINATIVE and ACCUSATIVE (case_manager.py:28-32); all other cases fall through to the bare assignment, making the handler registry a half-implemented abstraction layer.
findings.suggestion: Either complete all eight case handlers so the registry carries real policy, or collapse the handler registry entirely and route all case-setting through the model's own setter, removing the extra indirection.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/core/config.py defines two parallel configuration representations (LexiconConfig dataclass and LEXICON_OPENROUTER_CONFIG dict) that overlap in content.
evidence: LexiconConfig dataclass (config.py:77-142) stores base_api_url, default_model, fallback_models, retry config implicitly through timeout_seconds.

LEXICON_OPENROUTER_CONFIG plain dict (config.py:164-184) re-declares base_url, default_models per-task, retry_config with max_retries/base_delay/max_delay, and circuit_breaker — none of which appear in LexiconConfig.
Both objects are module-level; callers must know which representation to use, leading to implicit parameter bag duplication.
findings.suggestion: Merge retry_config and circuit_breaker fields into LexiconConfig and remove the LEXICON_OPENROUTER_CONFIG dict; expose a single typed config object to all consumers.

findings.summary: Neural structures in InsectModel are stored as plain dicts with heterogeneous keys rather than typed objects, creating an implicit parameter bag pattern.
evidence: _initialize_neural_structures() (base.py:120-210) builds dicts with keys 'type', 'activity', 'learning_rate', 'memory_capacity', 'case_assignment', 'connections', 'spatial_map', 'heading_direction', 'odor_responses', 'motion_detection', 'color_processing', 'motor_commands', 'rhythm_generators' — each structure having a different key set.

_update_neural_priorities() (base.py:446-474) must check both hasattr(structure, 'priority') and isinstance(structure, dict) to handle whichever form a structure takes, proving the dict bag causes defensive branching downstream.
process_sensory_input() (base.py:476-524) calls structure.process_input() on dict objects that have no such method, meaning the code will raise AttributeError at runtime on every sensory input call.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a NeuralStructure dataclass or Protocol with typed fields (activity, case_assignment, connections) and a process_input method; replace dict construction with typed instantiation.

findings.summary: InsectModel maintains its own self.current_case alongside the inherited self._case from Model, creating two parallel case-tracking mechanisms.
evidence: InsectModel.transform_case() (base.py:327-363) updates self.current_case but never calls super().case setter, so self._case (from Model) diverges from self.current_case immediately after the first transform.

InsectModel._select_action_by_case() (base.py:563-585) branches on self.current_case, while Model.update() (model.py:174-202) branches on self._case — two dispatch tables reading from two different attributes that can hold different values.

InsectModel.__init__ sets self.current_case = initial_case (base.py:92) and also calls super().__init__() which defaults self._case to Case.NOMINATIVE (model.py:41), meaning they start in sync only by accident.
findings.suggestion: Remove self.current_case entirely; override the case property setter in InsectModel to run the precision/priority updates, and read self.case everywhere.

findings.summary: Case-specific _update_* methods in Model and ActiveInferenceModel have docstrings and inline comments that restate what the method name already communicates.
evidence: model.py:205 _update_nominative docstring: 'Update for NOMINATIVE case: model as active agent generating predictions.' — identical information is in the Case enum comment and the method name.
active_inference.py:395 _update_nominative inline comment: '# Generate predictions without observations' above a call to self.likelihood(self.posterior_means) — the code expresses this unambiguously.
active_inference.py:418-428 _update_accusative: comment '# In ACCUSATIVE case, the model receives parameter updates' precedes two lines that call update_posterior, which communicates the same thing.
active_inference.py:479-480: '# In INSTRUMENTAL case, model serves as a computational tool' echoes the docstring on the same method from three lines earlier.
findings.suggestion: Remove inline comments that mirror the method name or the docstring. Keep only comments that explain non-obvious decisions (e.g., why learning_rate is 0.1, why samples=5).

findings.summary: InsectActiveInferenceModel implements core active-inference methods as stubs returning zeros or random values, while carrying full docstrings implying real behavior.
evidence: _predict_observations() (base.py:787-795): full docstring, body is 'return np.zeros(10)  # Placeholder'.

_compute_expected_free_energy() (base.py:851-862): two-paragraph docstring explaining forward models and belief propagation, body returns 'np.random.rand(5) * 0.5' with comment '# Placeholder: random expected free energy values'.
beliefs dict (base.py:745-748): three entries all marked '# Placeholder'.
self.expected_free_energy = np.zeros(5)  # Placeholder (base.py:752).
findings.suggestion: Either implement the methods or mark the class explicitly as abstract/NotImplemented so callers cannot instantiate it expecting correct free energy semantics.

findings.summary: Fine-grained info-level logging on routine operations (initialization, parameter updates, state changes) floods the log with implementation details rather than domain events.
evidence: model.py:72 logs every case change at INFO with model name, id, old and new value — emitted on every model.case assignment.
model.py:163 logs every set_parameters() call with the key list at INFO.
active_inference.py:94 logs 'Initialized ActiveInferenceModel ...' at INFO in __init__.
base.py:118 logs 'Initialized {species} model with case {initial_case}' at INFO.
base.py:358 logs 'Transformed case: {old} -> {target}' at INFO, duplicating the model.py:72 log that fires through the inherited setter path.
active_inference.py:319 and 343 log posterior update values at DEBUG on every single Bayesian update step.
logging.basicConfig is called at module import level in src/core/model.py:8, overriding any logging configuration set by callers.
findings.suggestion: Downgrade routine per-object lifecycle events (init, parameter set, single case change) to DEBUG. Reserve INFO for domain-significant events. Remove the module-level basicConfig call from model.py.

findings.summary: _apply_case_transformation in ActiveInferenceModel has four elif branches that execute pass with a comment, making them structurally identical dead code.
evidence: active_inference.py:171-179: precision_focus branches for 'parameters', 'inputs', and 'outputs' all resolve to pass with a comment stating what 'will be handled' elsewhere.
Only the 'likelihood' branch (nominative) runs actual code (multiplies likelihood_precision). The other three branches exist solely to look structurally complete.
This is a pattern of defensive scaffolding — empty branches written to suggest symmetry rather than encode behavior.
findings.suggestion: Remove the empty elif branches. If they are intended future extension points, raise NotImplementedError or use a TODO comment at the top of the method rather than empty conditional arms.

findings.summary: _update_instrumental calls self.update_posterior(observations, learning_rate) with two arguments, but update_posterior only accepts one.
evidence: update_posterior signature (active_inference.py:265): def update_posterior(self, observation: np.ndarray) -> Dict[str, Any] — one parameter.
_update_instrumental (active_inference.py:515): self.update_posterior(observations, learning_rate) passes learning_rate as a second positional argument.
This would raise TypeError at runtime whenever the INSTRUMENTAL case invert path is exercised.
findings.suggestion: Add learning_rate as an optional parameter to update_posterior and wire it into the simplified update path, or remove the argument from the _update_instrumental call.

findings.summary: Model.update() uses an eight-arm if/elif chain to dispatch to case methods rather than a data-driven dispatch table, despite a data-driven table already existing in InsectModel._select_action_by_case().
evidence: model.py:185-202: eight elif branches, each doing self._update_{casename}(data) — structurally a dispatch table written as a conditional chain.
base.py:573-585: InsectModel._select_action_by_case() correctly uses a dict {Case.X: self._select_x_action} and a .get() lookup — the same pattern the parent class could use.
The inconsistency means new cases require edits in two places in different styles.
findings.suggestion: Replace the if/elif chain in Model.update() with a dict dispatch table keyed on Case values, consistent with the pattern already used in InsectModel.

findings.summary: The three-level class chain Model -> ActiveInferenceModel -> InsectModel -> InsectActiveInferenceModel adds depth without clear separation of responsibilities at each layer.
evidence: InsectModel extends ActiveInferenceModel which extends Model. InsectActiveInferenceModel then extends InsectModel, creating four inheritance levels.

InsectActiveInferenceModel introduces compute_free_energy() and update_beliefs() — but ActiveInferenceModel (two levels up) already implements free_energy() and update_posterior() for the same concepts.
InsectModel.__init__ hard-codes n_states=5 default_parameters (base.py:81-87), bypassing ActiveInferenceModel's prior_means/prior_precision constructor parameters that exist for exactly this purpose.
The fourth level (InsectActiveInferenceModel) re-declares a 'beliefs' dict that duplicates the posterior_means/posterior_precision already managed by the grandparent class.
findings.suggestion: Flatten to two levels: a domain-agnostic ActiveInferenceModel base and a species-parameterized InsectModel. Move active-inference extensions into the base class rather than into a fourth subclass.

findings.summary: _validate_case_transformation defines a transition graph where every case can reach every other case, making the validation trivially vacuous.
evidence: base.py:377-387: every Case key maps to a list containing all seven other Cases.

A comment at base.py:376 states 'Made more flexible to allow realistic case transitions' — indicating the constraint was previously tighter and was loosened to pass tests rather than to model domain structure.
The function always returns True for any (current_case, target_case) pair, making it dead validation code that adds call overhead without enforcement.
findings.suggestion: Either define a meaningful sparse transition graph that encodes real insect behavioral constraints, or remove the validation function and the try/except wrapping in transform_case() if all transitions are valid by design.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/core/config.py mixes configuration data with a DeviceContext runtime utility and torch environment setup, violating single-responsibility.
evidence: config.py:27-73 defines configure_torch_environment() and DeviceContext — both are runtime device management concerns unrelated to configuration persistence.
config.py:14-24 suppresses two PyTorch CUDA warnings at module import time, meaning any module importing LexiconConfig silently alters the global warning filter.
LexiconConfig itself (lines 77-142) does not reference torch at all, confirming the torch utilities are colocated by accident rather than design.
findings.suggestion: Move configure_torch_environment and DeviceContext to a src/lexicon/core/device.py module. Move warning suppression into that module or into application entry points, not a config import.

findings.summary: Two parallel, incompatible Model base classes exist: src/core/model.py and src/models/base.py, creating an unresolvable ownership split.
evidence: src/core/model.py defines Model with full active-inference semantics (free_energy, update, case_history, precision_weights, connections, _apply_case_transformation hook).

src/models/base.py defines a second Model class with a stripped interface (only id, name, _case, data_buffer) and no overlap with the core version except the Case enum, which it re-imports from src/core/model.

tests/conftest.py imports Case from src.models.base, while tests/cases/test_case_manager.py imports both Model and Case from src.models.base; tests/transformations/test_case_transformations.py imports Model from src.core.model. The same abstraction has two independent source-of-truth files with different public contracts.

src/cases/case_manager.py must accept either variant silently; the CaseManager.calculate_free_energy method falls back to a hardcoded 1.0 rather than raising, concealing the incompatibility at runtime (confirmed at line 146 of the test: assert result == 1.0).

28 files in src/ import from src.core.model; only 1 imports from src.models.base within src/, but 15 test files import from src.models.base, meaning the test suite and production code do not share the same Model type.
findings.suggestion: Designate src/core/model.py as the sole Model source. Migrate the stripped src/models/base.py to a thin re-export shim (from src.core.model import Model, Case) and delete all duplicate implementation. Update all test imports to src.core.model.

findings.summary: pytest.ini permanently ignores three test paths (tests/pomdp/test_cases, tests/models/test_linear_regression.py, tests/models/linear_regression_cases), leaving significant coverage gaps that are hidden from CI.
evidence: pytest.ini addopts contains: --ignore=tests/pomdp/test_cases --ignore=tests/models/test_linear_regression.py --ignore=tests/models/linear_regression_cases
The comment in pytest.ini describes the reason as 'unimplemented modules or optional dependencies', meaning these are not flaky tests being temporarily silenced — they are structurally broken.

tests/models/linear_regression_cases/ contains 8 case-specific test files (nominative_case.py through vocative_case.py) covering what appears to be the core linear regression use case across all 8 CEREBRUM cases; these are entirely excluded.
tests/pomdp/test_cases/ contains test_nominative.py, test_ablative.py, test_locative.py, test_vocative.py — four case-specific POMDP scenario tests, all excluded.
The excluded files number at least 12+ test modules. There is no tracking issue, no xfail, no skip decorator — the exclusion is invisible at the individual test level.
findings.suggestion: Replace blanket --ignore flags with per-test @pytest.mark.skip(reason='...') or xfail decorators so the coverage gap is visible in test reports. Fix the underlying import/dependency issues that made the ignores necessary.

findings.summary: InsectModel (src/models/insect/base.py) duplicates case-tracking state parallel to the parent Model class rather than delegating.
evidence: InsectModel.__init__ sets self.current_case = initial_case (line 92) alongside self.case_history = [] (line 107), duplicating fields that already exist in the parent Model as self._case and self._case_history.

InsectModel.transform_case (line 337–355) reads and writes self.current_case directly, bypassing the parent Model.case setter and its _apply_case_transformation hook entirely. The parent class case setter (with logging and history tracking) is never triggered.
The parent's self._case_history and InsectModel's self.case_history are maintained independently, giving divergent histories for the same object.

get_state() (line 701) returns current_case from self.current_case, not from self._case — meaning the base Model.get_state() and InsectModel.get_state() would report different values for the same instance.
findings.suggestion: Remove self.current_case and self.case_history from InsectModel. Override _apply_case_transformation() to perform insect-specific transitions when the parent case setter fires. Use self.case (the parent property) throughout.

findings.summary: Example modules (thermostat.py, animal_agent.py) use try/except ImportError to switch between relative and absolute imports, exposing package structure decisions as runtime behavior.
evidence: thermostat.py lines 13-26 perform two separate try/except ImportError blocks: one for core.model and one for transformations.case_transformations.
animal_agent.py lines 19-24 repeats the same pattern for core.model.

6 such patterns were found across src/examples/ alone. The try/except construct means the import path actually taken depends on how Python is invoked, not on a declared package boundary. Import errors unrelated to package structure (e.g., a typo in a downstream import) are silently swallowed and then a fallback import is attempted, delaying failure and obscuring root cause.

animal_agent.py lines 28-38 embed a runtime .git-marker walk to determine project_root, hardcoding 'src/tests/example_outputs' as a side-effecting path at module import time. This creates a side effect (os.makedirs) at import, which breaks test isolation.
findings.suggestion: Fix the package to be installed editably (pip install -e .) via pyproject.toml and remove all try/except import fallbacks. Move the OUTPUT_DIR setup out of module-level scope and into a function or __main__ guard.

findings.summary: src/core/model.py and multiple other src/ library modules call logging.basicConfig() at module-level, hijacking the host application's logging configuration.
evidence: src/core/model.py line 8: logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, ...) is called unconditionally at module load. This is the highest-importer file in the project (57 importers), meaning every consumer has its logging configuration overridden on first import.
At least 13 additional src/ files call logging.basicConfig() at module scope or inside top-level setup code (confirmed via grep).
pytest.ini configures log_cli and log_file handlers; the repeated basicConfig calls during test collection will produce redundant or conflicting handler registrations.
findings.suggestion: Replace all logging.basicConfig() calls in library/src code with logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) and a NullHandler. Reserve basicConfig to __main__ entry points only.

findings.summary: CaseManager.calculate_free_energy silently returns 1.0 when no handler or model method is available, making the fallback indistinguishable from a correct computation.
evidence: The test at line 146 of test_case_manager.py asserts result == 1.0 and labels this 'Default fallback', confirming that the production code path for an unimplemented model returns a magic number rather than raising.

The base Model.free_energy() raises NotImplementedError (src/core/model.py line 123), but the CaseManager wraps the call and returns 1.0 when the model's free_energy raises, losing the signal entirely.
Callers of calculate_free_energy have no way to distinguish a genuine free energy of 1.0 from an absent implementation, making the method contract untrustworthy.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 1.0 fallback. Raise a descriptive ValueError or re-raise NotImplementedError when no free energy implementation exists. If a default is genuinely needed, make it explicit (e.g., a keyword parameter default_value=None with a caller-side check).

findings.summary: Model.update() in src/core/model.py uses a manual 8-branch if/elif chain to dispatch to case-specific update methods instead of a dispatch table.
evidence: Lines 185-202 of src/core/model.py contain: if self._case == Case.NOMINATIVE: ... elif self._case == Case.ACCUSATIVE: ... (8 branches for 8 cases).

The fallback at line 202 returns {"status": "error", ...} for an unknown case, but Case is an exhaustive Enum — this branch is unreachable. If a new case is added to the Enum, the dispatch chain silently returns the error dict rather than raising AttributeError.

All 8 default case-update methods (_update_nominative through _update_vocative) have identical bodies returning a dict with only status and data_received keys, providing no differentiation. They exist purely as override hooks but consume vertical space.
findings.suggestion: Replace the if/elif chain with a dispatch dict: _case_dispatch = {Case.NOMINATIVE: self._update_nominative, ...} and call _case_dispatch[self._case](data). This is O(1), exhaustive by construction, and makes missing cases raise KeyError immediately.

findings.summary: InsectModel._initialize_neural_structures() calls np.random.rand() without seeding at object construction time, making model instances non-deterministic across test runs.
evidence: Lines 135, 148, 161, 165, 172, 173, 184, 198 of src/models/insect/base.py each call np.random.rand() to initialize neural structure activity arrays (mushroom_body, central_complex, antennal_lobe, optic_lobe, subesophageal_ganglion, ventral_nerve_cord).
There is no random seed parameter in InsectModel.__init__ and no call to np.random.seed() before initialization.

Tests in tests/models/test_insect_models.py (e.g., test_insect_model_initialization) that assert on neural structure properties will receive different floating-point arrays on every run, making numerical assertions fragile.
findings.suggestion: Add a random_seed: Optional[int] = None parameter to InsectModel.__init__. When provided, call np.random.default_rng(random_seed) and pass it through _initialize_neural_structures to replace bare np.random.rand() calls.

findings.summary: The two abstract critical methods of Model (free_energy and predict) have no tests against any concrete subclass in the executed test suite.
evidence: test_model_free_energy_abstract (test_model.py line 115) only confirms that Model().free_energy() raises NotImplementedError — it does not test any subclass implementation.
test_model_predict_abstract (test_model.py line 205) similarly only tests the abstract stub.
No test file in tests/core/ imports ActiveInferenceModel, ThermostatModel, or AnimalAgent to exercise a real free_energy or predict implementation.
tests/examples/test_environment.py covers only the Environment class. No test covers ThermostatModel or AnimalAgent end-to-end active inference loops.

ActiveInferenceModel (10 importers, the next most-imported critical module) has one test file test_active_inference_pomdp.py — but it is a POMDP-scenario file, not a unit test of the ActiveInferenceModel interface itself.
findings.suggestion: Add a concrete subclass fixture in tests/core/ (e.g., a MinimalModel that returns a fixed free_energy float and a predict array). Use it to test that the case setter triggers _apply_case_transformation, that update() dispatches correctly, and that free_energy() returns the expected type.

findings.summary: Tests in tests/cases/ and tests/conftest.py instantiate the stripped src.models.base.Model rather than the canonical src.core.model.Model, meaning they do not test the class with 57 production importers.
evidence: tests/conftest.py line 8: from src.models.base import Case

tests/cases/test_case_manager.py line 15: from src.models.base import Model, Case — so all CaseManager tests use the stripped Model (no free_energy, no connections, no _apply_case_transformation, no precision_weights).

The CaseManager in production accepts src.core.model.Model instances (28 production importers). The test suite for CaseManager exercises it only with src.models.base.Model instances, which have a different interface.

TestCalculateFreeEnergy asserts result == 1.0 because src.models.base.Model has no free_energy() method at all, so the fallback fires — this test therefore does not cover the CaseManager's integration with the real Model.free_energy() path.
findings.suggestion: Change all test imports for Model to src.core.model.Model. Remove src.models.base.Model entirely (see dual_model_class finding). Re-run TestCalculateFreeEnergy against a real subclass so the fallback path is not the only path tested.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/core/engine.py (the main pipeline orchestrator) has zero test coverage in the executed test suite.
evidence: Grepping all test files for 'lexicon', 'LexiconEngine', or 'engine' finds only llm/test_openrouter_functionality.py, which tests OpenRouterAnalysisEngine, not the LEXICON pipeline.
pytest.ini testpaths includes tests/ and beyond_cerebrum/src/tests/ — there is no tests/lexicon/ directory.

src/lexicon/core/engine.py imports OpenRouterClient at module level and calls load_dotenv at module scope, meaning any test that imports it would require live credentials or mocking — but no such test exists.
The engine.py file also calls os.makedirs and glob operations at function scope with side effects, making isolation-free testing structurally difficult.
findings.suggestion: Create tests/lexicon/ with unit tests that mock OpenRouterClient and dotenv loading. At minimum, test that LexiconEngine initializes with a config, that its pipeline methods return expected shapes, and that errors from the LLM layer surface as LexiconError rather than raw exceptions.
